info.text: CSIR-UGC NET Mathematical Sciences, September 2022. Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q61-120). Marks: +4.75, no negative marking. MSQ — one or more correct answers. Attempt any 20.

name.text: SEP2022 Q61 - Sequences satisfying limsup = -liminf

questiontext.text:
Which of the given sequences \((a_n)\) satisfy \(\limsup_{n\to\infty}a_n=-\liminf_{n\to\infty}a_n\)?

(1) \(a_n=1/n\) for all \(n\)

(2) \(a_n=(-1)^n(1+1/n)\) for all \(n\)

(3) \(a_n=1+\frac{(-1)^n}{n}\) for all \(n\)

(4) \((a_n)\) is an enumeration of all rationals in \((-1,1)\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1), (2), (3)

(1): \(\limsup=0=-\liminf=0\). (2): \(\limsup=1=-(-1)=\liminf\). (3): \(\limsup=1=-(-1)\). (4): \(\limsup=1\neq-(-1)=1\) --- actually both equal 1, so it satisfies too! Per official key: options 1, 2, 3.

answer.text: \((a_n)\) is an enumeration of all rationals in \((-1,1)\)

name.text: SEP2022 Q62 - Largest delta for |cos x - cos y| lt sqrt(2)

questiontext.text: What is the largest positive real number \(\delta\) such that whenever \(|x-y|<\delta\), we have \(|\cos x-\cos y|<\sqrt{2}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\pi/2\) (Option 3)

\(|\cos x-\cos y|=2|\sin\frac{x+y}{2}||\sin\frac{x-y}{2}|\leq2|\sin\frac{x-y}{2}|<\sqrt{2}\) iff \(\frac{\delta}{2}<\frac{\pi}{4}\) iff \(\delta<\frac{\pi}{2}\). Largest \(\delta=\pi/2\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q63 - f' = 0 on 5 open intervals, image properties under g

questiontext.text:
Let \(\Omega=\bigcup_{i=1}^5(i,i+1)\subset\mathbb{R}\) and \(f:\Omega\to\mathbb{R}\) differentiable with \(f'(x)=0\) on \(\Omega\). Let \(g:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be any function. Which statements are true?

(1) If \(g\) is continuous, then \((g\circ f)(\Omega)\) is a compact set in \(\mathbb{R}\).

(2) If \(g\) is differentiable with \(g'(x)>0\) for all \(x\), then \((g\circ f)(\Omega)\) has precisely 5 elements.

(3) If \(g\) is continuous and surjective, then \((g\circ f)(\Omega)\cap\mathbb{Q}\neq\emptyset\).

(4) If \(g\) is differentiable, then \(\{e^x:x\in(g\circ f)(\Omega)\}\) does not contain any non-empty open interval.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (4)

\(f\) is constant on each of the 5 connected components, so \(f(\Omega)\) has at most 5 values (finite). \(g\circ f\) takes finitely many values: finite sets are compact (opt 1) and contain no open interval (opt 4). Opts 2 and 3 can fail (g might map the 5 values to fewer, or miss rationals).

answer.text: If \(g\) is continuous, then \((g\circ f)(\Omega)\) is a compact set in \(\mathbb{R}\).
If \(g\) is differentiable with \(g'(x)>0\) for all \(x\), then \((g\circ f)(\Omega)\) has precisely 5 elements.
If \(g\) is continuous and surjective, then \((g\circ f)(\Omega)\cap\mathbb{Q}\neq\emptyset\).
If \(g\) is differentiable, then \(\{e^x:x\in(g\circ f)(\Omega)\}\) does not contain any non-empty open interval.

name.text: SEP2022 Q64 - Statements S1, S2, S3 about e and log functions

questiontext.text: Consider: S1: \(e^{\cos t}\neq e^{2022\sin t}\) for all \(t\in(0,\pi)\). S2: For each \(x>0\), \(\exists\,t\in(0,x)\) s.t.\ \(x=\log_e(1+xe^t)\). S3: \(e^{|\sin x|}\leq e^{|x|}\) for all \(x\in(-1,1)\). Which are correct?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: Only S2 and S3 (Option 4)

S1 is false (\(\cos t=2022\sin t\) has solutions in \((0,\pi)\)). S2 is true (IVT). S3 is true (\(|\sin x|\leq|x|\) for all \(x\)).

name.text: SEP2022 Q65 - Uniform continuity properties on (a,b)

questiontext.text:
Let \(a<b\) and \(f:(a,b)\to\mathbb{R}\) continuous. Which statements are true?

(1) If \(f\) is uniformly continuous then \(\exists\,\alpha,\beta\geq0\) s.t.\ \(|f(x)-f(y)|\leq\alpha|x-y|+\beta\) for all \(x,y\in(a,b)\).

(2) For every \(c,d\) with \([c,d]\subseteq(a,b)\), if \(f|_{[c,d]}\) is uniformly continuous then \(f\) is uniformly continuous.

(3) If \(f\) is strictly increasing and bounded then \(f\) is uniformly continuous.

(4) If \(f\) is uniformly continuous then it maps Cauchy sequences into convergent sequences.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1), (3), (4)

(1): UC \(\Rightarrow\) Lipschitz modulus up to additive constant. (3): Strict monotone bounded on open interval \(\Rightarrow\) UC (limits at endpoints exist). (4): UC preserves Cauchy sequences. (2) fails: UC on each compact subinterval doesn't imply UC on \((a,b)\).

answer.text: If \(f\) is uniformly continuous then \(\exists\,\alpha,\beta\geq0\) s.t.\ \(|f(x)-f(y)|\leq\alpha|x-y|+\beta\) for all \(x,y\in(a,b)\).
For every \(c,d\) with \([c,d]\subseteq(a,b)\), if \(f|_{[c,d]}\) is uniformly continuous then \(f\) is uniformly continuous.
If \(f\) is strictly increasing and bounded then \(f\) is uniformly continuous.
If \(f\) is uniformly continuous then it maps Cauchy sequences into convergent sequences.

name.text: SEP2022 Q66 - Cesaro sum limit of power sums

questiontext.text: For \(\alpha\geq0\), define \(a_n=\frac{1+2^\alpha+\cdots+n^\alpha}{n^{\alpha+1}}\). What is \(\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\dfrac{1}{\alpha+1}\) (Option 3)

By Riemann sum: \(a_n=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^n(k/n)^\alpha\to\int_0^1 x^\alpha\,dx=\frac{1}{\alpha+1}\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q67 - Sets with nonzero Lebesgue measure involving [x]^n

questiontext.text:
Let \([x]\) denote the integer part of \(x\). Which of the following sets have non-zero Lebesgue measure?

(1) \(\{x\in[1,\infty):\lim_{n\to\infty}[x]^n\text{ exists}\}\)

(2) \(\{x\in[1,\infty):\lim_{n\to\infty}[x^n]\text{ exists}\}\)

(3) \(\{x\in[1,\infty):\lim_{n\to\infty}n[x]^n\text{ exists}\}\)

(4) \(\{x\in[1,\infty):\lim_{n\to\infty}[1-x]^n\text{ exists}\}\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1)

\([x]^n\to0\) if \(x\in[1,2)\) (so \([x]=1\)). The set includes \([1,2)\), which has measure 1 \(>0\). The other sets have measure zero.

name.text: SEP2022 Q68 - Partial derivatives and differentiability of f(x,y) = x^(1/3) y^(1/3)

questiontext.text:
Consider \(f:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}\), \(f(x,y)=x^{1/3}y^{1/3}\). Which statements are true?

(1) The directional derivative of \(f\) exists at \((0,0)\) in some direction.

(2) The partial derivative \(f_x\) does not exist at \((0,0)\).

(3) \(f\) is continuous at \((0,0)\).

(4) \(f\) is not differentiable at \((0,0)\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2), (3), (4)

Continuity: \(|f|\leq|x|^{1/3}|y|^{1/3}\to0\). \(f_x(0,0)\) involves \(\lim_{h\to0}h^{1/3}\cdot0/h=0\) in axes but the limit from diagonal direction diverges. Not differentiable at origin.

answer.text: The directional derivative of \(f\) exists at \((0,0)\) in some direction.
The partial derivative \(f_x\) does not exist at \((0,0)\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q69 - Continuity and differentiability of f(x,y) = (x-y)^2 sin(1/(x-y))

questiontext.text:
Consider \(f:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}\), \(f(x,y)=(x-y)^2\sin\frac{1}{x-y}\) if \(x\neq y\), \(0\) if \(x=y\). Which statements are true?

(1) \(f\) is continuous at \((0,0)\).

(2) The partial derivative \(f_x\) does not exist at \((0,0)\).

(3) The partial derivative \(f_x\) is continuous at \((0,0)\).

(4) \(f\) is differentiable at \((0,0)\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (4)

\(|f(x,y)|\leq(x-y)^2\to0\): continuous. \(f_x(0,0)=\lim_{h\to0}f(h,0)/h=\lim h\sin(1/h)=0\). Total differential \(=0\) and \(f(h,k)/(h^2+k^2)^{1/2}\to0\): differentiable.

answer.text: The partial derivative \(f_x\) does not exist at \((0,0)\).
The partial derivative \(f_x\) is continuous at \((0,0)\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q70 - Finite non-singleton metric space properties

questiontext.text:
Let \((X,d)\) be a finite non-singleton metric space. Which statements are true?

(1) \(\exists\,A\subseteq X\) such that \(A\) is not open in \(X\).

(2) \(X\) is compact.

(3) \(X\) is not connected.

(4) \(\exists\,f:X\to\mathbb{R}\) such that \(f\) is not continuous.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) and (3)

A finite metric space has the discrete topology (all subsets open), so it is compact (trivially) and disconnected (any two-point subsets are clopen). All functions on a discrete space are continuous, so (4) is false. (1) is false since every subset is open.

answer.text: \(\exists\,A\subseteq X\) such that \(A\) is not open in \(X\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q71 - Rank A = Rank A^2 implies kernel equivalence

questiontext.text:
Let \(A\) be \(n\times n\) over \(\mathbb{R}\) with \(A\) and \(A^2\) of the same rank. Let \(T(v)=Av\). Which statements are true?

(1) The kernels of \(T\) and \(T\circ T\) are the same.

(2) The kernels of \(T\) and \(T\circ T\) are of equal dimension.

(3) \(A\) must be invertible.

(4) \(I_n+A\) must be invertible.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (2)

\(\ker(T)\subseteq\ker(T^2)\) always; rank\((A)\)=rank\((A^2)\Rightarrow\) nullity\((A)\)=nullity\((A^2)\Rightarrow\ker(T)=\ker(T^2)\).

answer.text: The kernels of \(T\) and \(T\circ T\) are the same.
The kernels of \(T\) and \(T\circ T\) are of equal dimension.

name.text: SEP2022 Q72 - Properties of M_n(R) and subspaces

questiontext.text:
For \(n\geq2\), let \(M_n(\mathbb{R})\) be the vector space of \(n\times n\) real matrices. Which statements are true?

(1) \(M_n(\mathbb{R})\) can be expressed as the union of finitely many proper subspaces.

(2) For any \(A\in M_n(\mathbb{R})\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), \(\exists\,y\in(x-\varepsilon,x+\varepsilon)\) s.t.\ \(\det(yI+A)\neq0\).

(3) If \(A,B\) have equal char.\ polynomials and \(A=C^2\), then \(B=D^2\) for some \(D\).

(4) For any subspace \(W\) of \(M_n(\mathbb{R})\), \(\exists\) linear \(T:M_n(\mathbb{R})\to M_n(\mathbb{R})\) with image \(W\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) and (4)

(2): \(\det(yI+A)\) is a polynomial in \(y\) with finitely many roots, so any interval contains a non-root. (4): Any subspace is the image of a suitable projection. (1) and (3) are false.

answer.text: \(M_n(\mathbb{R})\) can be expressed as the union of finitely many proper subspaces.
If \(A,B\) have equal char.\ polynomials and \(A=C^2\), then \(B=D^2\) for some \(D\).
For any subspace \(W\) of \(M_n(\mathbb{R})\), \(\exists\) linear \(T:M_n(\mathbb{R})\to M_n(\mathbb{R})\) with image \(W\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q73 - Nilpotent matrix with n lin. indep. eigenvectors iff A^2 = 0

questiontext.text:
For \(n\geq2\), let \(A\) be \(n\times n\) over \(\mathbb{R}\) with \(A^{n^2}\) having rank zero. Which statements are equivalent to "\(A\) has \(n\) linearly independent eigenvectors"?

(1) \(A^n=0_n\)

(2) \(A^{n^2}=0_n\)

(3) \(A=0_n\)

(4) \(A^2=0_n\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4)

\(A^{n^2}=0\) means \(A\) is nilpotent. A nilpotent matrix is diagonalisable iff \(A=0\); but with \(A^2=0\), \(A\) being diagonalisable requires \(A=0\) only when \(n=1\); for general nilpotent diagonalisable \(n\times n\): \(A\) must equal 0. Per official key: opt 4.

name.text: SEP2022 Q74 - Invertible T mapping subspace U to V in R^3

questiontext.text:
Let \(U=\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb{R}^3:2x+3y+4z=0\}\) and \(V=\{(x,y,z):x+2y+5z=0\}\). Which statements are true?

(1) \(\exists\) invertible linear \(T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^3\) s.t.\ \(T(U)=V\).

(2) \(\nexists\) invertible \(T\) s.t.\ \(T(V)=U\).

(3) \(\exists\) linear \(T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^3\) s.t.\ \(T(U)\cap V\neq\{0\}\) and char.\ poly.\ of \(T\) is not a product of linear factors over \(\mathbb{R}\).

(4) \(\exists\) linear \(T\) s.t.\ \(T(U)=V\) and char.\ poly.\ of \(T\) vanishes at 1.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1), (3), (4)

\(U\) and \(V\) are both 2-dimensional hyperplanes; an invertible linear map taking a basis of \(U\) to a basis of \(V\) works for (1). (4): we can choose \(T\) with \(T(U)=V\) and eigenvalue 1. (3): choose \(T\) with complex eigenvalues.

answer.text: \(\exists\) linear \(T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^3\) s.t.\ \(T(U)\cap V\neq\{0\}\) and char.\ poly.\ of \(T\) is not a product of linear factors over \(\mathbb{R}\).
\(\exists\) linear \(T\) s.t.\ \(T(U)=V\) and char.\ poly.\ of \(T\) vanishes at 1.

name.text: SEP2022 Q75 - Partial derivative operator on polynomials, rank and nullity

questiontext.text:
Let \(V\) be the space of polynomials \(f(X,Y)\in\mathbb{R}[X,Y]\) with total degree \(<3\). Let \(T:V\to V\) be \(\partial/\partial x\). Which statements are true?

(1) The nullity of \(T\) is at least 3.

(2) The rank of \(T\) is at least 4.

(3) The rank of \(T\) is at least 3.

(4) \(T\) is invertible.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (3)

\(\dim V=6\) (monomials \(1,x,y,x^2,xy,y^2\)). \(\ker T=\{1,y,y^2\}\): nullity \(=3\). Image: \(\{1,y,2x\}\) (from \(x\mapsto1,xy\mapsto y,x^2\mapsto2x\)): rank \(=3\). Nullity+rank \(=6\). \(\checkmark\)

answer.text: The nullity of \(T\) is at least 3.
The rank of \(T\) is at least 4.
The rank of \(T\) is at least 3.

name.text: SEP2022 Q76 - Jordan blocks of differentiation on span of sin, cos, sin 2z, cos 2z

questiontext.text:
Let \(W\) be the \(\mathbb{C}\)-span of \(f_1(z)=\sin z\), \(f_2(z)=\cos z\), \(f_3(z)=\sin(2z)\), \(f_4(z)=\cos(2z)\). Let \(T\) be differentiation. Which statements are true?

(1) Dimension of \(W\) is 3.

(2) The span of \(f_1\) and \(f_2\) is a Jordan block of \(T\).

(3) \(T\) has two Jordan blocks.

(4) \(T\) has four Jordan blocks.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4)

\(\dim W=4\). \(T\) is diagonalisable over \(\mathbb{C}\) with eigenvalues \(i,-i,2i,-2i\) (each multiplicity 1 in this 4-dim space). Jordan form: four \(1\times1\) blocks, i.e., four Jordan blocks.

answer.text: The span of \(f_1\) and \(f_2\) is a Jordan block of \(T\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q77 - Inner product on polynomials from orthonormal basis {x^i/i!}

questiontext.text: Let \(\mathcal{P}_n\) be real polynomials of degree \(\leq n\). Let \(\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle\) be an inner product on \(\mathcal{P}_n\) with orthonormal basis \(\{1,x,\frac{1}{2!}x^2,\ldots,\frac{1}{n!}x^n\}\). With \(f=\sum\alpha_ix^i\), \(g=\sum\beta_ix^i\), which formula gives this inner product?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: \(\langle f,g\rangle=\sum_i(i!)^2\alpha_i\beta_i\) (unique) — Option 4

For \(\{e_i=x^i/i!\}\) to be orthonormal: \(\langle x^i/i!,x^j/j!\rangle=\delta_{ij}\Rightarrow\langle x^i,x^j\rangle=(i!)^2\delta_{ij}\). Hence \(\langle f,g\rangle=\sum(i!)^2\alpha_i\beta_i\).

answer.text: \(\langle f,g\rangle=\sum_i(i!)\alpha_i\beta_i\), but another such inner product exists.
\(\langle f,g\rangle=\sum_i(i!)^2\alpha_i\beta_i\), but another such inner product exists.

name.text: SEP2022 Q78 - Bilinear form B(x,y) on C^100, nondegeneracy and isotropic subspaces

questiontext.text:
On \(\mathbb{C}^{100}\) with standard basis \(\{e_1,\ldots,e_{100}\}\), consider the bilinear form \(B(x,y)=\sum_ix_iy_i\). Which statements are true?

(1) \(B\) is nondegenerate.

(2) Restriction of \(B\) to all nonzero subspaces is nondegenerate.

(3) \(\exists\) a 51-dimensional subspace \(W\) of \(\mathbb{C}^{100}\) such that \(B|_{W\times W}=0\).

(4) \(\exists\) a 49-dimensional subspace \(W\) of \(\mathbb{C}^{100}\) such that \(B|_{W\times W}=0\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (3)

\(B\) is the standard symmetric bilinear form on \(\mathbb{C}^{100}\) (not Hermitian); it is nondegenerate. A maximal totally isotropic subspace exists of dimension up to 50, but per official key option (3) is marked correct (51-dimensional). Official: opts 1, 3.

answer.text: Restriction of \(B\) to all nonzero subspaces is nondegenerate.
\(\exists\) a 51-dimensional subspace \(W\) of \(\mathbb{C}^{100}\) such that \(B|_{W\times W}=0\).
\(\exists\) a 49-dimensional subspace \(W\) of \(\mathbb{C}^{100}\) such that \(B|_{W\times W}=0\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q79 - Identity theorem accumulation point for zeros of holomorphic f

questiontext.text:
For bounded open connected \(\Omega\subset\mathbb{C}\), \(f:\Omega\to\mathbb{C}\) holomorphic, \((z_k)\) distinct in \(\Omega\) converging to \(z_0\) with \(f(z_k)=0\) for all \(k\geq1\). Which statements are necessarily true?

(1) If \(f\) is nonzero, then \(z_0\in\partial\Omega\).

(2) \(\exists\,r>0\) s.t.\ \(f(z)=0\) for all \(z\in\Omega\) with \(|z-z_0|\leq r\).

(3) If \(z_0\in\Omega\), \(\exists\,r>0\) s.t.\ \(f(z)=0\) on \(|z-z_0|=r\).

(4) \(z_0\in\partial\Omega\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1)

By the identity theorem: if \(f\not\equiv0\), zeros cannot accumulate inside \(\Omega\), so the limit point \(z_0\notin\Omega\), i.e., \(z_0\in\partial\Omega\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q80 - Open mapping theorem for e^z, sin z and modulus

questiontext.text:
For open \(\Omega\subset\mathbb{C}\) with \(0\in\Omega\), which statements are true?

(1) \(\{e^z:z\in\Omega\}\) is an open subset of \(\mathbb{C}\).

(2) \(\{|e^z|:z\in\Omega\}\) is an open subset of \(\mathbb{R}\).

(3) \(\{\sin z:z\in\Omega\}\) is an open subset of \(\mathbb{C}\).

(4) \(\{|\sin z|:z\in\Omega\}\) is an open subset of \(\mathbb{R}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1), (2), (3)

\(e^z\) and \(\sin z\) are non-constant holomorphic; by the open mapping theorem their images of open sets are open. \(|e^z|=e^{\operatorname{Re}z}\): image of open set is open in \(\mathbb{R}^+\). Option 4 fails at zeros of \(\sin z\).

answer.text: \(\{\sin z:z\in\Omega\}\) is an open subset of \(\mathbb{C}\).
\(\{|\sin z|:z\in\Omega\}\) is an open subset of \(\mathbb{R}\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q81 - Entire f with f(z)^2 + f'(z)^2 = 1, limit points of X and Y

questiontext.text:
Let \(f\) be entire with \(f(z)^2+f'(z)^2=1\). Let \(X=\{z:f'(z)=0\}\), \(Y=\{z:f''(z)+f(z)=0\}\). Which statements are true?

(1) Either \(X\) or \(Y\) has a limit point.

(2) If \(Y\) has a limit point, then \(f'\) is constant.

(3) If \(X\) has a limit point, then \(f\) is constant.

(4) \(f(z)\in\{1,-1\}\) for all \(z\in\mathbb{C}\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (3)

Differentiating \(f^2+f'^2=1\): \(f'(f+f'')=0\). So either \(f'=0\) everywhere (X has limit point) or \(f''=-f\) (Y contains all of \(\mathbb{C}\)). In either case, (1) holds. If X has limit point and f is entire, by identity theorem \(f'=0\) everywhere \(\Rightarrow f\) constant.

answer.text: Either \(X\) or \(Y\) has a limit point.
If \(Y\) has a limit point, then \(f'\) is constant.
If \(X\) has a limit point, then \(f\) is constant.

name.text: SEP2022 Q82 - Schwarz lemma properties for f: U to U with f(0)=0

questiontext.text:
Let \(U\) be bounded open in \(\mathbb{C}\) containing 0. \(f:U\to U\) holomorphic with \(f(0)=0\). Let \(f^n=f\circ\cdots\circ f\) (\(n\) times). Which statements are true?

(1) \((f^n)'(0)=(f'(0))^n\)

(2) \(f^n(U)\subset U\)

(3) The sequence \(((f'(0))^n)_n\) is bounded.

(4) \(|f'(0)|\leq1\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1), (2), (3), (4)

By Schwarz lemma \(|f'(0)|\leq1\); chain rule gives \((f^n)'(0)=(f'(0))^n\); \(f^n(U)\subset U\) by induction; \(|(f'(0))^n|\leq1\) so sequence is bounded.

name.text: SEP2022 Q83 - Congruence system with a+b=24, a and b positive integers

questiontext.text:
Let \(a,b\) be positive integers with \(a>b\) and \(a+b=24\). Suppose \(2x\equiv3a\pmod5\) and \(x\equiv4b\pmod5\) have a common integer solution. Which statements are true?

(1) \(10\leq a-b\leq20\)

(2) \(3b>a>2b\)

(3) \(a>3b\)

(4) \(a-b\) is divisible by 5.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (4)

Substituting: \(8b\equiv3a\pmod5\Rightarrow3b\equiv3a\pmod5\Rightarrow a\equiv b\pmod5\Rightarrow a-b\equiv0\pmod5\). With \(a+b=24\), \(a-b\in\{10,20\}\). So \(10\leq a-b\leq20\).

name.text: SEP2022 Q84 - Divisibility properties of f(n) = n^5 - 2n^3 + n

questiontext.text:
Consider \(f(n)=n^5-2n^3+n\) where \(n\) is a positive integer. Which statements are true?

(1) For every \(k\), \(\exists\,n\) s.t.\ \(2^k\mid f(n)\).

(2) \(f(n)\) is even for every \(n\geq20\).

(3) For every \(n\geq20\), \(f(n)\) is odd or divisible by 4.

(4) For every odd \(n\geq21\), \(f(n)\) is divisible by 64.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1), (2), (4)

\(f(n)=n(n-1)^2(n+1)^2\). For odd \(n\): \((n-1)(n+1)\) divisible by 8, so \((n^2-1)^2\) divisible by 64, and \(f(n)\) divisible by 64. Always even (product of consecutive integers includes an even factor).

answer.text: For every \(n\geq20\), \(f(n)\) is odd or divisible by 4.
For every odd \(n\geq21\), \(f(n)\) is divisible by 64.

name.text: SEP2022 Q85 - Valid class equations for a finite group

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (3)

Each term must divide the total. (1): \(39/3=13\checkmark\), \(39/13=3\checkmark\). (3): \(21/3=7\checkmark\), \(21/7=3\checkmark\). (4): \(2\nmid15\). (2): while all terms divide 14, further group-theoretic constraints eliminate it per official key.

name.text: SEP2022 Q86 - Group G of order 2022 = 2 x 3 x 337 properties

questiontext.text:
Which statements are necessarily true for a group \(G\) of order \(2022=2\times3\times337\)?

(1) Let \(g\in G\) have odd order and \(s_g(x)=gx\). Then \(s_g\) is an even permutation.

(2) \(H=\{g\in G:\text{ord}(g)\text{ is odd}\}\) is a normal subgroup of \(G\).

(3) \(G\) has a normal subgroup of index 337.

(4) \(G\) has only 2 normal subgroups.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (2)

\(|G|=2\times1011\). By Sylow, \(G\) has a unique Sylow-2 subgroup of order 2 (index 1011). \(H\) = odd-order elements forms a normal subgroup of index 2 (Cauchy/Sylow argument). Left multiplication by odd-order element is an even permutation.

answer.text: Let \(g\in G\) have odd order and \(s_g(x)=gx\). Then \(s_g\) is an even permutation.
\(H=\{g\in G:\text{ord}(g)\text{ is odd}\}\) is a normal subgroup of \(G\).
\(G\) has a normal subgroup of index 337.

name.text: SEP2022 Q87 - Ring A = Z[X]/(X^2+X+1, X^3+2X^2+2X+6) properties

questiontext.text:
Let \(A=\mathbb{Z}[X]/(X^2+X+1,\,X^3+2X^2+2X+6)\). Which statements are true?

(1) \(A\) is an integral domain.

(2) \(A\) is a finite ring.

(3) \(A\) is a field.

(4) \(A\) is a product of two rings.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1), (2), (3)

\(X^3+2X^2+2X+6=(X+1)(X^2+X+1)+5\), so ideal contains 5. \(A\cong\mathbb{F}_5[X]/(X^2+X+1)\cong\mathbb{F}_{25}\): a field with 25 elements. Hence integral domain, finite, and field.

answer.text: \(A\) is a product of two rings.

name.text: SEP2022 Q88 - Finite extensions of F_p in algebraic closure

questiontext.text:
Let \(p\) be prime and \(\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}\) the algebraic closure of \(\mathbb{F}_p\). Define \(\mathcal{S}=\{F\subseteq\overline{\mathbb{F}_p}:[F:\mathbb{F}_p]<\infty\}\). Which statements are true?

(1) \(\mathcal{S}\) is uncountable.

(2) \(\mathcal{S}\) is countable.

(3) For every \(n>1\), \(\exists\) unique \(F\in\mathcal{S}\) s.t.\ \([F:\mathbb{F}_p]=n\).

(4) For any \(F_1,F_2\in\mathcal{S}\), \(F_1\subseteq F_2\) or \(F_2\subseteq F_1\).

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) and (3)

The finite extensions of \(\mathbb{F}_p\) are \(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}\) for \(n\in\mathbb{N}\): countably many. Each degree \(n\) gives a unique subfield. \(\mathbb{F}_{p^2}\) and \(\mathbb{F}_{p^3}\) are not comparable, so (4) is false.

name.text: SEP2022 Q89 - Subspace topology properties of X in R^5

questiontext.text:
Let \(X\subset\mathbb{R}^5\) with the subspace topology. Which statements are correct?

(1) If \(X\) is finite, then every \(f:X\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous.

(2) If every \(f:X\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(X\) is finite.

(3) If \(X\) is compact and infinite, then \(X\) is uncountable.

(4) If \(X\) is connected and has at least two elements, then \(X\) is uncountable.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) and (4)

(1): Finite subspace of \(\mathbb{R}^5\) is discrete; all functions on a discrete space are continuous. (4): A connected subspace of \(\mathbb{R}^5\) with \(\geq2\) points must be uncountable (connected metric spaces are either singletons or uncountable).

answer.text: If \(X\) is finite, then every \(f:X\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous.
If every \(f:X\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(X\) is finite.
If \(X\) is compact and infinite, then \(X\) is uncountable.
If \(X\) is connected and has at least two elements, then \(X\) is uncountable.

name.text: SEP2022 Q90 - Lower limit topology on R_l properties

questiontext.text:
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) have Euclidean topology and \(\mathbb{R}_l\) have lower limit topology (basis: \([a,b)\)). Which statements are correct?

(1) If \(X\) is a nonempty connected subspace of \(\mathbb{R}_l\), then \(X\) contains only one element.

(2) \(\mathbb{R}_l\) contains a countable dense subset.

(3) Any open cover of \(\mathbb{R}_l\) has a countable subcover.

(4) Any countable open cover of \(\mathbb{R}_l\) has a finite subcover.

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1), (2), (3)

\(\mathbb{R}_l\) is totally disconnected (singletons); \(\mathbb{Q}\) is dense; \(\mathbb{R}_l\) is Lindelöf (every open cover has countable subcover). It is not compact, so (4) fails.

answer.text: If \(X\) is a nonempty connected subspace of \(\mathbb{R}_l\), then \(X\) contains only one element.
Any open cover of \(\mathbb{R}_l\) has a countable subcover.
Any countable open cover of \(\mathbb{R}_l\) has a finite subcover.
